topic.what: Systematic screening of Mexican traditional medicinal plants for anti-H. pylori antibacterial activity in vitro, identifying multiple species from Mesoamerican healing traditions with significant inhibitory effects.
topic.how: Ethanol and aqueous extracts from plants collected based on ethnobotanical surveys of traditional use for gastric/digestive complaints; MIC and disk-diffusion assays against H. pylori ATCC reference strain and clinical isolates; active extracts prioritized for further characterization.
topic.why_matters: Mesoamerican traditional medicine has a distinct pharmacopoeia shaped by pre-Columbian and mestizo healing knowledge largely unexplored against H. pylori; this screening reveals that indigenous GI remedy plants in Mexico have convergent anti-H. pylori utility, potentially via novel phytochemical scaffolds absent from European or Asian pharmacopoeias.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Proportion of screened plants with significant anti-H. pylori activity (MIC ≤ 500 µg/mL)
characteristics.value: Multiple species showed significant activity; hit rate higher than random plant panels, supporting ethnobotanical guidance value
characteristics.vs_baseline: Non-ethnobotanically guided plant screens typically report <10% hit rate
characteristics.evidence: MIC determinations against H. pylori for all screened species
characteristics.context: Plants selected based on traditional use for gastric complaints, enriching active discovery

characteristics.what_it_measures: MIC of most active plant extracts against H. pylori
characteristics.value: Best extracts achieved MIC in the 62.5–250 µg/mL range
characteristics.vs_baseline: Clarithromycin MIC against sensitive H. pylori (0.015–0.5 µg/mL)
characteristics.context: Plant extracts are crude; purified active compounds would be expected at lower MIC

characteristics.what_it_measures: Botanical family breadth of active species
characteristics.value: Active plants span multiple families (Asteraceae, Lamiaceae, and others), suggesting diverse chemical scaffolds
characteristics.context: Supports idea generation: distinct scaffolds may offer novel mechanisms

good_for: Discovery of novel anti-H. pylori phytochemicals from underexplored Mesoamerican flora
Ethnobotanical validation of Mexican traditional gastric remedies
Prioritization of species for phytochemical isolation and drug discovery
Cross-cultural comparison: Mexican plants vs Old World anti-H. pylori remedies
not_for: Direct clinical application without isolation, safety, and efficacy data
Identifying specific active compounds (crude extract screen only)
Patients outside the ethnobotanical context without species availability
requires: Bioactivity-guided fractionation to identify active phytochemicals
In-vivo validation in H. pylori animal models
Ethnobotanical documentation of preparation methods and dosing traditions

novelty: First systematic anti-H. pylori screen of plants specifically selected from Mexican ethnobotanical tradition for gastric use
Documents Mesoamerican pharmacopoeia as a source of anti-H. pylori leads, underrepresented in global natural products literature
Demonstrates that ethnobotanical pre-selection enriches antimicrobial hit rate, supporting rational ethnopharmacology
open_problems: Active compounds in the most potent extracts have not been identified
Activity against antibiotic-resistant H. pylori clinical strains not tested
In-vivo validation absent; gastric bioavailability in humans unknown
Traditional preparation methods (decoction, infusion) vs ethanol extract potency comparison needed
